title: Minor Magic Items
description: Small enchanted objects for loot, gifts, purchases, or discoveries — useful curiosities and minor wonders without overwhelming power.

entries.result: A candle that burns for exactly one hour before self-extinguishing and relighting the next night at the same time.
A brass compass that always points toward the nearest door, window, or other opening rather than north.
A small mirror that shows the viewer's reflection as it was ten years ago.
A coin that, when flipped, always lands on the side the flipper secretly wants. Useless for gambling. Useful for decisions.
A quill that writes in invisible ink visible only by candlelight.
A ring that keeps the wearer's hands exactly at body temperature regardless of ambient cold or heat.
A stone that hums quietly when held in running water.
A small bottle that refills with fresh water once per day at dawn.
A folding knife that stays sharp indefinitely and never rusts, but also cannot be sharpened further than its original edge.
A thread spool that produces thread matching any color pressed against it.
A pair of spectacles that reveal whether written text is a copy or an original.
A small clay pot that keeps anything stored in it at the temperature it was when sealed.
A rope that ties perfect knots when commanded but cannot be made to tie knots by hand.
A comb that detangles any hair instantly but always leaves it slightly damp.
A thimble that, when worn, prevents the wearer from ever pricking their finger. On anything. For any reason.
A lantern that casts light only the holder can see. Appears dark to all others.
A book whose pages are blank to anyone but the original owner, who sees normal text.
A belt buckle that adjusts the belt to a perfect fit regardless of the wearer's size.
A pair of boots that mute the sound of the wearer's footsteps on stone only.
A small bird carved from bone that whistles when dawn breaks, regardless of weather or enclosure.
A key that opens any lock it fits, but changes its shape to fit only the last lock it opened.
A hat that makes the wearer feel confident. Whether it makes others perceive confidence is debated.
A dried flower that never fully loses its color or scent.
A small hammer that drives nails perfectly straight with one blow, but cannot be used to remove them.
A jar of ink that dries instantly and cannot be smudged.
A handkerchief that, when unfolded, is always clean and dry regardless of how it was last used.
A monocle that identifies the monetary value of any object, expressed in the nearest local currency.
A copper coin with no markings that always returns to the pocket it was taken from by the following morning.
A spoon that detects poison by turning black. It also turns black in the presence of very strong spices, which is a known limitation.
A pair of gloves that prevent blisters and calluses while doing any kind of manual labor.
A small hourglass that runs backward and resets to full when turned right-side up.
A whetstone that sharpens blades twice as fast as normal but leaves them with a faint blue edge.
A fishing hook that never loses its bait but also never catches fish larger than the holder's hand.
A small bell that rings once when someone nearby tells a lie. The person lying cannot hear it.
A stick of charcoal that never runs out but draws only in the color of shadows.
A cup that chills any liquid placed in it to a comfortable drinking temperature.
A needle that threads itself when held near thread.
A cloak clasp that warns the wearer of approaching rain by vibrating gently.
A small painted portrait that blinks exactly once per day, always when the owner is looking away.
A wooden whistle that summons birds to nearby branches. They do not perform any task; they simply come.
A bookmark that, placed in any book, opens to that page from memory without being guided.
A coin purse that feels lighter than its contents. The weight is real; only the sensation changes.
A door handle that, when touched by a sleeping person's hand, prevents them from sleepwalking past it.
A small wax seal stamp that leaves an impression resistant to forgery. Each copy made from it looks slightly wrong in a way that experts notice.
A pouch that keeps herbs dry and fresh indefinitely.
A set of dice that roll randomly, as dice should, but remember the result of every roll ever made with them.
A pen that corrects the writer's spelling automatically. It has strong opinions about grammar and occasionally argues.
A pair of earrings that allow the wearer to hear whispered conversations at twice the normal distance.
A stick of wax that seals envelopes and documents with a bond that can only be broken by the intended recipient.
A thumb-sized owl carved from amber that orients itself toward north when placed on a flat surface.
A scarf that warms the wearer against cold wind but provides no protection from cold water.
A small lantern fueled by the user's breath that burns for an hour per deep breath without consuming air.
A pair of socks that keep feet dry in any terrain but make a faint squelching sound on every step.
A map that adds roads, rivers, and landmarks the owner discovers, but cannot be used to draw anything deliberately.
A flask that purifies any liquid poured into it, but removes the flavor along with the impurities.
A lock that cannot be picked but can be opened by humming any tune at the correct pitch.
A chalk stick that marks surfaces but cannot be erased except by the person who drew the mark.
A cloak that changes color to match the background if the wearer stands still for ten seconds.
A pair of tweezers that can grasp objects half the size of a normal tweezer's grip.
A ring with a gemstone that glows faintly when the wearer is underground.
A pair of sandals that keep the wearer's feet from burning on hot surfaces but still allow damage from sharp ones.
A small jar of salve that heals minor skin irritations instantly but smells strongly of mint.
A pewter cup that shows the drinker a single pleasant memory while they drink.
A pocket watch that keeps perfect time but cannot be wound. It stopped once, years ago, and has been running since.
A small portrait locket that shows the face of whoever the wearer is thinking about most strongly.
A coin that becomes warm to the touch in the presence of someone who wishes the holder harm.
A walking stick that will not slip on ice, mud, or wet stone, but slides freely on dry surfaces.
A small drum that, when beaten, causes anyone in earshot to feel a vague sense of impending good news.
A bar of soap that cleans without water. The lather appears and vanishes without moisture.
A pin cushion that catches any falling pin, needle, or small sharp object within a foot before it can hit the floor.
A ribbon that can be used to close wounds as effectively as a bandage but causes slight tingling.
A jar of paste that adheres any two non-living surfaces together permanently. It does not adhere to living skin.
A feather that writes in the air, the words dissolving after an hour.
A small ship in a bottle in which the ship moves as though sailing. It mirrors the weather of the nearest coast.
A set of measuring weights that are always perfectly calibrated, regardless of humidity or wear.
A signet ring that leaves an impression in any soft material it's pressed against, including stone.
A hat pin that, when removed, causes the wearer to feel a sudden urgency about something.
A tiny sealed bottle that, when shaken, produces a sound like distant waves.
A lens that magnifies script to any size the reader needs, intuiting the size without adjustment.
A small painted box that, when opened, produces a faint scent of the last memory its owner is trying to forget.
A set of lockpicks that unlock any lock they can physically reach, then break, reliably and completely, on the third use.
A cufflink that, when touched, allows the wearer to recall the last conversation they had in perfect detail.
A small piece of charcoal that reproduces any writing or drawing pressed against it.
A stone that, when placed under a pillow, causes the sleeper to remember their dreams in full detail.
A clasp knife with a handle that is always comfortable to hold regardless of temperature.
A pair of tongs that grip anything they are directed at without slipping, including things they should not be able to grip.
A small jar of invisible ink that becomes visible in firelight but not candlelight.
A ring that prevents the wearer from forgetting a name once heard.
A compass that points toward the nearest body of water rather than magnetic north.
A small bronze medallion that warms noticeably when the wearer is being followed.
A canteen that produces one cup of clean water per day, regardless of what is already inside it.
A folded cloth that, when shaken out, is always the right size to cover what the shaker intends to cover.
A small carving of a hand with its palm facing out that, hung on a door, prevents the door from being slammed.
A bottle stopper that seals any bottle airtight, regardless of the bottle's shape or size.
A pair of scissors that cut straight lines perfectly but cannot cut curves.
A mirror fragment that shows the room behind the viewer rather than the room in front.
A hollow ring that, when blown across, plays a different note depending on the direction of the wind.
A hat that prevents its wearer from being rained on directly, though those beside the wearer may be soaked.
A small book that, when opened to any page, contains useful but unsolicited advice about the reader's current situation.
A silver thimble that, when worn while sewing, causes the stitching to hold twice as long as it should under any conditions.
A coin that, when spun, slows as though in heavy air and takes twice as long to fall.
A glass marble that, held to the eye, shows the location of the nearest underground water source.
A small iron horseshoe that, when nailed above a door, prevents livestock from passing under the threshold. This is more useful than it sounds in some circumstances.
A clasp that attaches to any cloak and prevents the garment from catching on branches, doorframes, or other obstacles.
A small wooden figure that falls over when placed in a room with a secret door or hidden compartment.
A pen that writes only truth. If the writer attempts to write a lie, the pen produces an illegible scrawl.
A deck of cards with hand-painted faces. The card drawn first from the deck each morning is always relevant to the day.
A ring that makes the wearer's voice carry clearly in a noisy room without raising their volume.
A bronze fox figurine that orients its nose toward the nearest hidden thing.
A pouch of dried herbs that, burned, produces smoke that smells different to each person present — always the scent they associate with safety.
A walking stick that judges the safety of ground before the walker steps on it by tapping automatically.
A small box that produces a sound of rain when closed. Useful for sleeping. Useless in actual rain.
A belt that adjusts tightness on its own to prevent anything stored in pockets or at the waist from falling out.
A leather folder that keeps any documents stored in it from getting wet, torn, or creased.
A stone the size of a fist that, when placed in water, makes the water briefly glow blue.
A key that, when placed in any lock, indicates whether it will fit by growing warm if yes, cold if no.
A small pebble that, when held, lets the holder feel the direction of the nearest safe shelter.
A candle that burns without dripping and with no smoke.
A bottle that prevents any wine stored in it from turning to vinegar, regardless of how long it is kept.
A comb that, when run through hair before sleep, prevents nightmares. It doesn't produce good dreams — just empty, restful ones.
A ring with a red stone that pulses once whenever someone nearby is concealing a weapon.
A small knife with a handle of carved bone that always returns to the hand that dropped it before it hits the floor.
A jar of salve that removes ink stains from skin and fabric instantly but permanently lightens the area slightly.
A pendant that grows warm in the presence of magic that has been deliberately hidden.
A pair of reading glasses that translate one foreign language into the reader's native tongue when worn.
A small silver spade that breaks ground easily regardless of soil hardness, but is the size of a trowel and can't be scaled up.
A jar that preserves any food placed inside it without pickling, salting, or treatment.
A rope that, when commanded, ties itself into any specified knot.
A cloak hook that holds any weight hung on it without pulling from the wall.
A small brass weight that, when placed in any pan scale, reveals if the other pan has been tampered with.
A lantern that dims at exactly the right moment to not be seen from a distance, as though it reads the situation.
A piece of chalk that marks on surfaces visible only to children under the age of ten.
A hat that is always the right size for whoever is wearing it.
A travel cloak that dries in ten minutes regardless of how wet it gets.
A set of scales that weigh correctly only when both parties in a transaction agree to use them.
A small bronze disc that produces a sound like a struck bell when placed near magic that has recently been cast.
A bottle that, when opened near dying embers, produces a flame without tinder.
A set of earplugs that block loud sudden noises but allow normal conversation through.
A carved wooden fish that, hung in a fishing boat, prevents the craft from taking on water through the hull.
A pair of dice with an extra face each that always rolls a result the roller cannot predict.
A small bone disc engraved with a skull that causes animals to instinctively avoid its carrier.
A vial that, when opened, produces the smell of rain regardless of the weather outside.
A ring that, when worn, prevents the wearer from leaving fingerprints.
A jar of paste that, spread on wood, prevents it from rotting for a decade.
A compass needle that spins frantically in the presence of a deliberate deception rather than magnetic north.
A small copper cup that reveals the source of any liquid poured into it.
A pipe that, when smoked, produces smoke that forms shapes mirroring the smoker's current mood.
A pair of cufflinks that allow the wearer to go without sleep for one night per week without feeling the lack.
A small hourglass where the sand moves upward, counting forward rather than down.
A signet ring that, pressed into wax, reveals whether the letter inside has been previously read.
A coin that is always the most commonly used denomination in whatever region it's currently in.
A small knife that hums when pointed toward the person who last sharpened it, regardless of distance.
A cup that refuses to spill its contents regardless of how it is tilted.
A travel lantern that burns without fuel for exactly seven nights, then goes permanently dark.
A pen that produces ink in any color the writer imagines.
A small brass ear that, pressed to a wall, amplifies sound on the other side.
A ring that makes the sound of the wearer's voice slightly more pleasant.
A sieve that separates gold from dirt at ten times the speed of a normal pan.
A leather cord that can be knotted once for each promise made on it and unknots itself when the promise is kept.
A candleholder that prevents the candle from guttering in wind.
A small bag of seeds that always produce the same plant when planted, regardless of soil quality.
A ring that prevents the wearer from blushing.
A knife sheath that keeps any blade in it from rusting.
A blank card that, left in the presence of a person for an hour, develops a sketch of their face.
A small mirror that, held over a sleeping person, reveals whether they are dreaming.
A bone needle that can sew leather as easily as cloth.
A stick of sealing wax that, when broken, produces a sharp crack audible to the person who sent the letter, regardless of distance.
A brooch that keeps any cloak, scarf, or fabric attached to it from being removed by wind.
A pair of spectacles that allow reading in complete darkness by rendering text faintly luminous.
A small iron key that locks any door from the inside without a keyhole.
A canteen stopper that notifies the carrier when the canteen is less than a quarter full.
A pair of boots that, when walked backward in, erase the tracks of the wearer's forward steps.
A coin pouch that makes a sound of coins when squeezed regardless of whether it contains any.
A small clay figurine that cracks when the person it was made to resemble is seriously injured.
A length of thread that cannot be cut by anything except scissors it was originally packaged with.
A small bronze hand mirror that shows the reflection of the room an hour ago.
A jar of polish that, applied to boots, makes the wearer walk silently on dry stone for an hour.
A ring with a blue stone that turns red when any form of fire is burning within thirty feet.
A walking stick that, planted firmly, refuses to fall over regardless of how it is struck.
A piece of charcoal that produces a sound when rubbed across a surface if that surface has been written on before in the same place.
A cloak clasp shaped like an eye that opens when the wearer is being observed without their knowledge.
A small clay tile that glows softly when placed in an area where someone died within the last year.
A bottle with a wax seal that, when opened, lets out a single sigh — and then is simply a bottle.
A lock of hair tied with red cord that grows warm when the person it belonged to is nearby.
A wax tablet that erases cleanly but retains a ghost impression of the last thing written on it visible only to the original writer.
A pair of gloves that allow the wearer to feel heat but not to be burned by it.
A coin that, left on a closed eyelid, produces a vision of the most important thing that happened in the place the viewer is standing.
A glass bead that, held to the ear, produces the sound of the sea, regardless of the bead's origin.
A small carved cat that, placed near a mouse hole, prevents mice from entering but does not harm them.
A pendant that makes the wearer feel calm in situations that should cause panic. The feeling is real; the safety is not guaranteed.
A ring that keeps track of how many times the wearer has broken a promise and will not let the count be forgotten.
A carved stone fish that, placed in a barrel of water, keeps the water from stagnating.
A lantern that, rather than casting shadows, illuminates them slightly — making the dark areas of a room marginally more visible than the lit ones.
A small box of matches that each burn for exactly five minutes, evenly, with no smoke.
A ring that prevents the wearer's handwriting from being forged. Copies look plausible but fail any detailed examination.
A small carved eye that, hung near a door, records the face of everyone who passes through.
A short length of silk rope that, when tied around the wrist, prevents the wearer from speaking an outright lie for an hour. Omissions are permitted.
A clay cup that, filled with water, shows the reflection of the most recently deceased person the drinker knew.
A small bell jar that, when placed over any object, preserves it in exactly the state it was when covered.
A brass key that opens nothing but feels, whenever held, like it is about to.
